description: Publish the browser extension as a private npm package and bundle it as a CLI dependency so that `opentabs start` can auto-install the extension for users who install the CLI from npm

userStories.title: Prepare browser extension package.json for npm publishing
userStories.description: As a platform maintainer, I want the browser extension package.json to be publishable to npm, so that it can be consumed as a dependency by the CLI
acceptanceCriteria: platform/browser-extension/package.json has a `files` field that includes only the runtime files needed by Chrome: `manifest.json`, `dist/`, `side-panel/`, `offscreen/`, `icons/`
platform/browser-extension/package.json version matches other platform packages (will be bumped by publish.ts)
The `exports` field is updated if needed (currently only exports `./package.json` which is fine for resolution)
userStories.notes:
File: platform/browser-extension/package.json

Currently the package has no `files` field, no `publishConfig`, and version is `0.0.1` while other packages are at `0.0.32`. The version will be bumped by publish.ts, so just ensure it's a valid semver.

Add a `files` field to control what goes in the npm tarball. Only include the files needed by Chrome to load the extension:
```json
"files": [
  "manifest.json",
  "dist/",
  "side-panel/",
  "offscreen/",
  "icons/"
]
```

This excludes `src/`, `node_modules/`, `build-*.ts`, `tsconfig*`, `.storybook/`, `storybook-static/`, `CLAUDE.md`, and other dev-only files from the published tarball. The `files` field is the npm-standard way to do this (equivalent to `.npmignore` but more explicit).

Do NOT add `"private": true` — it must be publishable. Do NOT change the access level (it will be published as restricted/private via `bun publish --access restricted` in publish.ts).

userStories.title: Add browser extension to CLI dependencies and publish pipeline
userStories.description: As a platform maintainer, I want the browser extension published alongside other platform packages and listed as a CLI dependency, so that `import.meta.resolve('@opentabs-dev/browser-extension/package.json')` resolves when the CLI is installed from npm
acceptanceCriteria: scripts/publish.ts includes `platform/browser-extension` in the PACKAGES array, positioned BEFORE `platform/cli` in dependency order (since CLI depends on it)
The publish order is: shared → browser-extension → mcp-server → plugin-sdk → plugin-tools → cli → create-plugin
userStories.notes:
Two files to edit:

1. platform/cli/package.json — add `"@opentabs-dev/browser-extension": "workspace:*"` to the `dependencies` object (lines 26-33). This ensures npm installs the extension alongside the CLI.

2. scripts/publish.ts — add `'platform/browser-extension'` to the PACKAGES array (lines 77-84). It must come BEFORE `'platform/cli'` since the CLI depends on it. A good position is after `'platform/shared'` since the extension depends on shared. The updated order:
```typescript
const PACKAGES = [
  'platform/shared',
  'platform/browser-extension',
  'platform/mcp-server',
  'platform/plugin-sdk',
  'platform/plugin-tools',
  'platform/cli',
  'platform/create-plugin',
] as const;
```

After adding the dependency, run `bun install` to update the lockfile.

userStories.title: Fix setup.ts error message for missing extension
userStories.description: As a user installing the CLI from npm, I want the error message when the extension source is missing to give me actionable guidance, not tell me to run `npm run build from the project root`
acceptanceCriteria: The error message in setup.ts line 55 no longer says 'Run npm run build from the project root first'
The error message is actionable for an npm-installed user (e.g., suggests reinstalling the CLI or checking the installation)
The setup logic in resolveExtensionDir() still tries import.meta.resolve first, then falls back to monorepo-relative path
userStories.notes:
File: platform/cli/src/commands/setup.ts

Line 55: `throw new Error(\`Browser extension not found at ${extensionSrc}. Run npm run build from the project root first.\`);`

This message makes sense for monorepo contributors but is confusing for npm users. With the extension now bundled as a dependency (US-002), this error should only occur if the npm install is corrupted.

Change to something like:
```typescript
throw new Error(`Browser extension not found at ${extensionSrc}. Try reinstalling: npm install -g @opentabs-dev/cli`);
```

Also check the warning message in start.ts (around line 89-93) where the error is caught and displayed — it currently says:
```
Warning: Could not install extension: <error.message>
Restart opentabs start to retry extension installation.
```
This is fine as-is since it just passes through the error message.

userStories.title: Update CLAUDE.md documentation for extension publishing
userStories.description: As a platform contributor, I want the documentation to reflect that the browser extension is now published to npm and bundled with the CLI
acceptanceCriteria: The root CLAUDE.md publish order section mentions browser-extension in the correct position
The CLI CLAUDE.md mentions browser-extension as a dependency
The Architecture section or relevant docs note that the extension is distributed via npm as part of the CLI package
userStories.notes:
Files to update:

1. platform/cli/CLAUDE.md — in the 'Publishing Platform Packages' section, update the package list and publish order to include `@opentabs-dev/browser-extension`. Current text says:
   'shared → mcp-server → plugin-sdk → plugin-tools → cli → create-plugin'
   Change to:
   'shared → browser-extension → mcp-server → plugin-sdk → plugin-tools → cli → create-plugin'

2. Root CLAUDE.md — if the publish order or architecture description mentions the package list, update it there too. Search for 'publish' references.

Keep changes minimal and factual — just update the publish order and note the dependency relationship. Do NOT enumerate individual plugins or add excessive detail per CLAUDE.md guidelines.
